column2: The 1980 Recommended Renewable Resources Program is a U.S. Forest Service plan, prepared under the Forest and Rangeland Renewable Resources Planning Act of 1974 (as amended by the National Forest Management Act of 1976), guiding management of the Nation's forest and rangeland resources from 1981 through 2030. It covers four major Forest Service responsibilities—management of about 187 million acres of National Forest System lands, State and Private Forestry assistance, Research, and Human Resource Programs—organized around 12 program elements including recreation, wilderness, wildlife and fis…

column2: The Program affects users and communities nationwide, with the strongest effects in rural and nonmetropolitan areas dependent on Forest Service outputs—particularly timber-dependent communities in the Pacific Northwest and South, ranching communities in the West, and areas experiencing mineral development 'boom town' conditions. Other affected groups include nonindustrial private forest landowners, State forestry agencies, recreation users (including urban residents), county and local governments receiving federal payments, and consumers facing rising prices for wood, forage, and water produc…

Five Alternative Program Directions were evaluated: Alternative 1 (highest market and nonmarket outputs), Alternative 2 (minimum custodial program), Alternative 3 (moderate balanced multiple-use, similar to the 1975 Program), Alternative 4 (low NFS market outputs with high nonmarket emphasis and expanded State/private production), and Alternative 5 (continuation of current trends). A sixth alternative, 4a, was added in response to public comment to combine high nonmarket outputs with maintained commodity production through intensive management on a smaller commercial land base. The Recommende…

Both Program Bounds and most alternatives would meet Clean Water Act water quality goals by 2000–2005 and national ambient air quality standards by 1995, with short-term localized impacts from vegetation treatment, road building, and mining. Under the High Bound and higher-output alternatives, wildlife and fish habitat would improve significantly, with most threatened and endangered species populations stable or increasing, though old-growth-dependent species such as Sitka black-tailed deer and marten in Alaska would decline due to timber harvest. Lower-output alternatives reduce short-term d…

Public participation was extensive: a 1977 comment period generated over 2,000 pages of input, and review of the March 1979 draft documents drew more than 1,600 respondents through nationwide public meetings, with about 70 percent of written comments from unaffiliated individuals. Roughly 45 percent of respondents preferred one of the five alternatives, with Alternatives 3 and 4 most favored in the East, Alternative 3 in much of the West, and Alternative 1 strongly preferred in Alaska, while Alternatives 2 and 5 received the least support. Comments led to adding Alternative 4a, identifying ne…
